text.title: Alone in Patagonia
text.u: The idea of solo travel has always felt
intimidating to me. Not because I didn't
want to go, but because I wasn't sure
how I'd handle being
alone. What would it feel like to be
this far away from home with no one else
to share the experience
with, no one to laugh with in weird
moments, no one to reminisce with down
the road?
Just me, the road, and my
camera. Patagonia was the kind of place
I've dreamed about for years. Jagged
mountains, endless sky, wild weather,
and it felt like the right place for the
biggest solo trip of my life. Big enough
to get lost in, but quiet enough to
maybe hear something that I've been
missing.
My trip begins in Torres Delp Pine,
beneath the twisted granite of the horns
of Chile. This place has been on my
bucket list for as long as I can
remember, and the views were everything
I'd hoped
for. But even surrounded by that beauty,
I kept feeling the absence of something.
I've always believed that experiences
are better when they're shared. And
through my 20s, I traveled often with
close friends, sharing moments like
these together. But life changes, people
grow older, start families, chase
careers, and suddenly it's more
difficult to line everything up. That's
been one of the hardest parts of getting
older, realizing that if I keep waiting
for everything to align, I might miss
the moments I've spent years dreaming
about. Later that afternoon, I hiked up
to Miridor Condor, a rocky outcrop
overlooking the turquoise waters of
Logo.
It's a short, steep climb. And when I
reached the top, I realized I was
completely alone, not another person in
sight. And somehow that moment, so still
and surreal, felt even more powerful
because of
it. It made me realize that maybe part
of growing up is learning to hold both
things at once. the longing to share
something and the quiet strength of
being okay on your
own. And even though this trip feels
like a test of that idea, these few days
in Torres del Pine were truly a dream
come true.
[Music]
[Music]
About 8 hours up the road from Torres
Delp Pine, just across the border into
Argentina, are the iconic peaks of
Elshalt. It honestly feels like driving
straight into a postcard.
The weather is forever changing here in
Patagonia, so there's never really a
perfect time to get out on the trail.
You just kind of have to go for
it. The trail climbs slowly through a
quiet forest of Langga trees, then
opened up to a spectacular view of the
Rio de lostos valley
below. And somewhere along that trail, I
finally started to feel it. That quiet
I've been so unsure about going into
this trip. It didn't feel quite as
heavy. Being alone, I felt this strange
sense of creative freedom. There was no
one to keep up with, no plan I had to
stick to. It was just me and my camera.
That's all that
mattered. And then almost out of
nowhere, Fitzroy finally broke through
the clouds. It was like something out of
a dream towering above the autumn colors
below. It felt like one of those rare
moments you only get when you slow down
long enough to notice. The kind of thing
that reminds you why you came all this
way in the first place.
Sunrise on Fitzroy is truly something
else. The mountains here feel almost
alive, like a giant canvas catching the
first brush strokes of light. For a few
brief moments, everything glows. The
peaks shift from a deep blue to fire
orange, then fade into soft pinks and
golds. There's nothing to do in a moment
like that except be still. Just take it
all in.
[Music]
[Music]
On my final day in Patagonia, fresh snow
dusted the higher peaks, only to be
immediately swept away by the fierce
winds blowing off the ice field.
I wandered out into the wide open plains
outside of town, hoping to spot some
guanako. And man, was I in
luck. Guanakos are wild relatives of
llamas native to South America and
perfectly adapted to these harsh
environments. They travel in herds,
relying on each other for safety. Their
sharp eyes and quick movements helping
them evade predators like
pumas. Watching them move together, so
coordinated and so alert, I felt a quiet
kind of
awe. There's something about their herd
mentality, how they watch out for each
other, that really struck me. Out here
on my own, it reminded me how important
connection is. that even in solitude,
we're part of something bigger, whether
we realize it or
not. Traveling alone here wasn't always
easy. There were moments when the
silence felt overwhelming, when I wished
I had someone to share the struggle, the
quiet, or just that ridiculous
jaw-dropping view
with. I don't know if solo travel is
something that I'll ever love, but I do
know I'm walking away with a different
version of myself. One that's learned to
slow down, accept the messiness, and
show up even when it feels
uncertain. And maybe that's the point.
Maybe it's less about having all the
answers, and more about being willing to
step into the unknown anyway. And
honestly, that feels like enough.
Thank you so much for watching and I'll
see you next time.
